purpose: Written to reassure believers of their genuine fellowship with God, to counter a schismatic teaching that denied Christ's full humanity, and to stress the centrality of love and obedience.
historicalContext: The letter reflects a painful split within its community, apparently involving early proto-Gnostic ideas that questioned whether Jesus had truly come 'in the flesh.'
structure: Fellowship with God and walking in light (1-2)
Children of God and love for one another (3-4)

title: 2 John
author: Traditionally attributed to John the Apostle
authorNote: 2 John shares the same authorship discussion as 1 and 3 John. The author identifies only as 'the Elder,' which fuels ongoing scholarly debate over whether this refers to the apostle John, another early leader of the same name, or a figure within the Johannine community.
audience: Addressed to 'the elect lady and her children,' likely a symbolic reference to a specific local church and its members.
purpose: Written to warn against welcoming traveling teachers who denied Christ's incarnation, and to reaffirm the command to love one another.
historicalContext: The letter reflects concerns within the Johannine community about itinerant teachers spreading doctrine inconsistent with its understanding of Christ.
structure: Greeting and the call to love (1-6)

title: 3 John
author: Traditionally attributed to John the Apostle
authorNote: 3 John shares the same authorship discussion as 1 and 2 John, with the author again identifying only as 'the Elder,' leaving open whether this is the apostle John or another leader connected to the Johannine community.
audience: Gaius, an individual leader or member within a local church.
purpose: Written to commend Gaius for his hospitality toward traveling teachers, and to criticize Diotrephes for refusing to cooperate and asserting improper control over the church.
historicalContext: The letter offers a rare glimpse into leadership tensions and questions of authority among early house churches, as different local figures vied for influence.

title: Jude
author: Traditionally attributed to Jude, brother of James
authorNote: Authorship of Jude is debated. Some scholars accept the traditional attribution to Jude, brother of James (and by extension a half-brother of Jesus), noting the modest, non-self-aggrandizing way the author identifies himself. Others view the letter as pseudonymous, pointing to its close literary relationship with 2 Peter and a probable later composition date.
dateWritten: c. 65-90 CE; precise dating is disputed.
audience: A general Christian audience facing infiltration by false, morally permissive teachers.
purpose: Written to urge believers to 'contend for the faith' against false teachers who had entered the community and were undermining its moral and doctrinal integrity.
historicalContext: The letter closely parallels chapter 2 of 2 Peter, suggesting a literary relationship between the two texts in one direction or another, and addresses a community perceived as being led astray by teachers promoting license.

title: Revelation
author: Traditionally attributed to the apostle John
authorNote: Early tradition identifies the author as the apostle John, though the text itself simply names its author 'John.' Some scholars, noting stylistic differences from the Gospel and letters of John, propose a different early Christian figure known as 'John of Patmos,' distinct from the apostle. This remains a genuinely debated question.
dateWritten: Most scholars date it to around AD 95, during the reign of the Roman emperor Domitian; a minority propose an earlier date around AD 68-70, under Nero.
audience: Seven churches in Roman Asia Minor facing pressure to compromise their faith under Roman imperial religion.
purpose: To encourage persecuted and pressured Christian communities with a vision of God's ultimate victory over evil and the promise of a renewed creation.
historicalContext: Written during a period of tension between the early church and Roman imperial cult practices, the book uses apocalyptic imagery common in Jewish literature of the era to address political and spiritual pressures facing its first readers.
structure: The Fall of Babylon and Final Victory (chapters 17-20)
The New Heaven and New Earth (chapters 21-22)
